## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hi, today I want to talk about the creative process and how you can get your work to be made more easily, be more powerful, and be more like you. My name is Nicholas Wilton. I'm a fine artist. We're standing in my studio in Sausalito. I've been painting my entire life, making art my whole life. I've worked with thousands of people, helping them bring their work out to make their work more potent and more powerful and more like themselves. This happened quite by accident. It actually happened through my own struggle early on. I was always trying to make work and I struggled frankly. I struggled a lot trying to make my work powerful and actually just make work that I liked. I know a lot of you can relate to this. On one day you make something that's really great and sometimes that can be done so easily. Then you'll work two more days and then you make a few more things and those are terrible. Then you feel like giving up and then you get frustrated and then you make something else and then that looks really great. Then you do something you think you hate and then two weeks later you like it. It's so confusing and it's so difficult. I went to an amazing art school. I went to Art Center College of Design. I aced it there and I had incredible teachers and I've worked really hard. I've been able to thrive as an artist and I'm thankful for that. I love it. I love my life. I love the creative process. It really didn't get going. It really didn't happen for me until I started to really pay attention to the process of my work and what was actually happening. I started to take notes basically when things were working and when things weren't working. Over a decade, several 20 years of writing down why I was working, how did this happen? How could I be so good on Wednesday and so bad on Thursday? What came out of this was a system, was a set of principles. I call them the art life principles. I've been teaching these now for about 20 years and this has evolved over time through a lot of trial and error. I've distilled it down so these ideas once understood. Now it's not just like ABC. There's some complexity here. If you can get these principles, if you can understand these principles, you can transform your work. I'm amazed when I first started teaching this 20 years ago that I could take somebody who was vaguely, minorly interested in art, had maybe no experience at all. Given these principles, they could actually make work that starts feeling incredibly powerful and looks and feels like their own work. I'm not interested in teaching a set of techniques or teaching a way of working certain kind of materials.

like way more powerful than color or texture. So that's just something to kind of keep in mind. What I'm showing you, what I'm sharing with you is really powerful. And I was never really shown this. ## [09:03 - 12:06]

It's not something that's new, but kind of thinking it in this way is a slightly different twist. Design, the way I just find it, is just simply the arrangement of shapes on a picture. So if I have a picture, now this could be a photograph or it could be a sculpture or whatever. It doesn't matter, but I'll say picture because that's kind of where I'm starting from. If I have a picture and there's a mark on it and then I have another mark down here and I have something over here, this is the design. It's the arrangement of whatever shapes you get. Now, I've made abstract little shapes here, but this could be a picture of something. This could be a dog. This could be a telephone pole. This could be a forest. It doesn't really matter what kind of work you do. It could be a photograph. It could be a realistic photograph of a person. But when you can clearly see the shapes of things and you can look at in some total, that's the design of the work. We're interested in making design really, really strong. Strong design and strong design comes from differences. Differences that we can see. So, a really obvious one is scale, different sizes, different shapes. So, if I take this and I make this a little circle and I make this much bigger. So, I'm making this different than this in several ways. This is a square. So, it's kind of hard edged. And this is a circle and it's kind of round. This is also much bigger. And what happens when we look at a picture or a sculpture or a photograph or anything in our world, we look at part of it. And then instantly, within a millisecond, we look at the other part. We go to the next noticeable thing. And when we see it, it registers whether it's different or not. So, if we start here and we go all the way down here, when this is different, it wakes us up. That's what makes it kind of come alive for us. So, if I want to add something else here, if I add it a little square here and we go from here over to this square, it's a square. But it's also kind of the same size and it kind of feels the same. It's not really that hard edged. It's not really square. It's kind of, it is a square but it's kind of rounded on its corners. It feels kind of like that. So, in order to improve this design, all I have to do is make this different, you know, more different.
